seoTitle: How to Create a Scheduled Flow in Power Automate
metaDescription: Learn how to build a scheduled cloud flow in Power Automate, configure recurrence triggers, and automate recurring tasks.
intro: Scheduled flows in Microsoft Power Automate let you run automations automatically at set times or intervals, without any manual intervention. This guide walks through creating one, from setting up the recurrence trigger to testing your finished workflow.

steps.heading: Create a scheduled cloud flow
steps.body: Start a new flow in Power Automate and choose the scheduled cloud flow option to begin building your automation.

steps.heading: Configure the recurrence trigger
steps.body: Set how often the flow should run, whether daily, weekly, or monthly, and specify the exact time it should fire.

steps.heading: Add workflow actions
steps.body: Build out the actions the flow should perform each time it runs, such as sending emails, generating reports, or syncing data.

steps.heading: Test and monitor the flow
steps.body: Run a test to confirm the flow executes correctly, then check the run history to monitor future scheduled executions.

steps.heading: Apply it to real business tasks
steps.body: Use the same approach to automate recurring processes like backups, notifications, approvals, and data synchronization.

faq.question: How often can a scheduled flow run?
faq.answer: You can configure recurrence to run daily, weekly, monthly, or at custom intervals depending on your needs.

faq.question: Can I change the schedule after creating the flow?
faq.answer: Yes, you can go back into the flow and edit the recurrence trigger settings at any time.

faq.question: How do I check if a scheduled flow ran successfully?
faq.answer: Power Automate keeps a run history for each flow where you can review whether each scheduled run succeeded or failed.

faq.question: What kinds of tasks are good candidates for scheduled flows?
faq.answer: Recurring tasks like reports, email notifications, file management, backups, and data synchronization are well suited to scheduling.
